name: Algebraic Closed-Form DDM with Softmax Action Selection
description: Uses the exact closed-form solutions for first-passage time statistics of the Wiener process (Bogacz et al., 2006) to analytically compute per-action choice probability and expected decision time, avoiding simulation of stochastic trajectories. Each action's drift rate is derived from perceptual proximity to resources. The DDM-predicted accuracy P_i and mean decision time T_bar_i are combined with a learned Q-value into a composite action value V_i. Actions are selected via softmax sampling over these composite values. This formulation implements postulates P2 (drift rate reflects stimulus quality) and P3 (speed-accuracy trade-off controlled by boundary separation) in an algebraically tractable way, making it computationally fast while preserving the theoretical grounding of the DDM. After each step, the chosen action's Q-value is updated via a simple temporal-difference rule.

variables.name: choice_probability
variables.description: Closed-form probability that a DDM accumulator for action i would hit the upper boundary, given drift rate v_i, boundary a, and diffusion coefficient sigma. Derived from the Wiener first-passage solution.

variables.name: expected_decision_time
variables.description: Mean first-passage time for the accumulator for action i, derived from the Wiener process closed-form solution. Used as a time-cost penalty.

variables.name: composite_action_value
variables.description: Combined value of action i: accuracy-weighted benefit minus time cost plus learned utility. The decision criterion for action selection.

variables.name: action_selection_probability
variables.description: Softmax probability of selecting action i, derived from composite values V_i and inverse temperature beta.

variables.name: learned_action_utility
variables.description: Running estimate of expected reward from taking action i. Updated via temporal-difference learning after each step.

variables.name: drift_rate
variables.description: Drift rate for action i computed from perceptual signal. Positive values indicate evidence favoring the action; higher magnitude means stronger evidence.

variables.name: perceptual_signal
variables.description: Raw perceptual signal for action i derived from resource proximity or resource presence on cell.

parameters.name: boundary_separation
parameters.source: Ratcliff & McKoon (2008); typical value for moderate caution

parameters.name: softmax_inverse_temperature
parameters.source: Controls exploration-exploitation trade-off; knowledge backbone default from Run 542c7e41

parameters.name: learning_rate
parameters.source: Standard RL learning rate for Q-value updates

parameters.name: drift_rate_scaling
parameters.source: Maps perceptual signal to drift rate units; linked to postulate P2

parameters.name: time_cost_penalty
parameters.source: Penalizes slow decisions; implements speed-accuracy trade-off per postulate P3

parameters.name: accuracy_weight
parameters.source: Relative importance of DDM-predicted accuracy in composite value

parameters.name: utility_weight
parameters.source: Relative importance of learned reward signal in composite value

parameters.name: zero_drift_guard
parameters.source: Numerical guard for v_i near zero in Eq. 3 (L'Hopital limit); standard numerical practice

rules.description: Drift rate from perception: scales the perceptual signal by k_v to produce a drift rate for each action.
rules.pseudocode:
for each action i:
  v_i = k_v * s_i

rules.description: Closed-form choice probability: exact DDM accuracy from Wiener first-passage analysis (Bogacz et al., 2006, Eq. 2.8). Assumes unbiased starting point z = a/2.
Expected decision time: closed-form mean first-passage time (Bogacz et al., 2006, Eq. 2.10). Uses numerical guard for near-zero drift.
Composite action value: combines DDM-predicted accuracy (higher is better), a time-cost penalty (slower decisions are penalized), and a learned utility term.
rules.pseudocode: V_i = w_P * P_i - lambda_t * T_bar_i + w_Q * Q_i

rules.description: Softmax action selection: converts composite values into a probability distribution over actions using the Boltzmann softmax with inverse temperature beta.
rules.pseudocode:
max_V = max(V_i for all i)  # numerical stability
pi_i = exp(beta * (V_i - max_V)) / sum(exp(beta * (V_j - max_V)) for all j)
R = sample from categorical distribution with probabilities pi_i

rules.description: Q-value update: simple temporal-difference update for the chosen action only.

decision_logic.description: Per-step decision: compute perceptual signals, derive drift rates, evaluate closed-form DDM accuracy and decision time, form composite values, and sample action via softmax. No internal simulation loop needed — all computations are algebraic. After reward feedback, update the Q-value for the chosen action.
pseudocode: food_on_cell = any(f['x'] == pos[0] and f['y'] == pos[1] for f in food_list)
# Step 2: Compute perceptual signals s_i for each action
d_i = min Manhattan distance from neighbor to any food (large number if no food)
# Step 3: Compute drift rates via R1
v_i = k_v * s_i  # for each action i
# Step 4: Compute DDM accuracy P_i via R2
P_i = 1.0 / (1.0 + exp(-2.0 * v_i * a / sigma**2))  # for each action i
# Step 5: Compute expected decision time T_bar_i via R3
# Step 6: Compute composite value V_i via R4
V_i = w_P * P_i - lambda_t * T_bar_i + w_Q * Q_i  # for each action i
pi_i = exp(beta * (V_i - max_V)) / sum(exp(beta * (V_j - max_V)) for all j)
# Step 9 (in update method): Update Q_chosen via R6

perception_to_variables.food_on_cell: any(f['x'] == x and f['y'] == y for f in perception['resources']['food'])

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action → ConsumeEffect with reward 1.0; move/stay actions yield reward 0.0

expected_behaviors.description: Agent moves toward food: actions pointing toward nearby food get higher drift rates, higher P_i, and thus higher V_i, making them more likely under softmax.
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: place food at (5,5), agent at (0,0) → over 50 steps, assert agent's distance to food decreases on average

expected_behaviors.description: Agent eats when on food: the eat action gets s_eat = 1.0 when food is on cell, producing high drift and high P_eat, dominating the softmax.
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: place agent on food cell → run decide() 100 times → assert eat is chosen > 70% of the time

expected_behaviors.description: Speed-accuracy trade-off: increasing boundary separation 'a' makes P_i closer to 1.0 but increases T_bar_i, shifting the balance in V_i. Higher lambda_t favors faster (lower-confidence) actions.
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: compare action distributions with lambda_t=0.01 vs lambda_t=1.0 → higher lambda favors actions with lower T_bar

expected_behaviors.description: Q-learning adapts over time: initially Q_i = 0 for all actions, but after repeated eat rewards, Q_eat increases, further biasing selection toward eat when on food.
No internal simulation loop: decision is computed in O(n_actions) algebraic operations, not iterative accumulation. Faster than Formulation 1.

references: Ratcliff, R. (1978). A theory of memory retrieval. Psychological Review, 85(2), 59-108. DOI: 10.1037/0033-295X.85.2.59
Ratcliff, R., & McKoon, G. (2008). The diffusion decision model: Theory and data for two-choice decision tasks. Neural Computation, 20(4), 873-922. DOI: 10.1162/neco.2008.12-06-420
Ratcliff, R., & Rouder, J. N. (1998). Modeling response times for two-choice decisions. Psychological Science, 9(5), 347-356. DOI: 10.1111/1467-9280.00067
Bogacz, R., Brown, E., Moehlis, J., Holmes, P., & Cohen, J. D. (2006). The physics of optimal decision making. Psychological Review, 113(4), 700-765. DOI: 10.1037/0033-295X.113.4.700
